title: WHO SEARO Rabies PEP Clinician Intake Checklist
description: Flat checklist of every clinical question derived from the WHO SEARO Rabies PEP Decision Tree, structured as question nodes for a clinician-facing intake workflow.

source.name: WHO SEARO Rabies Post-Exposure Prophylaxis Decision Tree
source.citation: WHO South-East Asia. Decide with Confidence: Rabies Post-Exposure Prophylaxis Decision Tree.

design_notes: Each question node maps to one decision diamond or grouped multi-check from the WHO SEARO flowchart.
Questions appearing in multiple flowchart branches (e.g. animal availability, vaccination status) are deduplicated here; routing logic belongs in the decision-tree schema.
response_type 'binary_yn' is used where the source flowchart shows only Y/N branches; 'ternary_ynu' where Y/U or N/U combined branches appear, indicating that 'unknown' is a valid clinical response.
group_id links questions that the source diagram evaluates together as a multi-any-yes block; group_evaluation describes the trigger logic.
footnote_refs maps each question to the numbered source footnote(s) that contextualise it for the clinician.
inline_notes captures verbatim callout-box text printed adjacent to the corresponding node in the source diagram.
source_phrase preserves the question text exactly as it appears in the source diagram.
workflow_notes: If exposure was to a bat, PEP for WHO wound category III should be started immediately.
If, during subsequent visits, the time since exposure exceeds 10 days and the animal is still alive, PEP can be discontinued.

footnotes.1: Dogs, cats and domestic ferrets being healthy 10 days after exposure present no rabies risk and PEP is not indicated. The infectious periods of other animals are not well characterized, and a more conservative 14-day clinical investigation is recommended.
footnotes.2: Such as bites to the head, neck, face, genitals and hands.
footnotes.3: Clinical signs of rabies in an animal include hypersalivation, paralysis, lethargy, unprovoked abnormal aggression (e.g. biting two or more people or animals and/or inanimate objects), abnormal vocalization, diurnal activity of nocturnal species. Hydrophobia is not a sign of rabies in dogs.
footnotes.4: This risk assessment is made at one point in time with the available information. If new information is provided or the status of the animal changes, PEP might be indicated.
footnotes.5: Wash the wound thoroughly with copious amounts of water and soap for 15 min and apply an antiseptic. This is a life-saving practice especially for immunocompromised patients.
footnotes.6: When feasible, the RABV neutralizing antibody response should be determined 2–4 weeks after vaccination to assess whether an additional dose of vaccine is required.
footnotes.7: This includes Pre-Exposure Prophylaxis (PrEP), previous PEP, or patients beyond the 7th day of PEP.
footnotes.8: If PrEP or previous PEP was received: shorten the current vaccination schedule accordingly.
footnotes.9: Patient with multiple bites, deep wounds, bites to highly innervated parts of the body (such as head, neck, face, genitals and hands), severe immunodeficiency, bites from an animal with probable (clinically) or confirmed (laboratory) rabies, exposure to a bat (bite, scratch or exposure of mucous membrane).

questions.source_phrase: Skin is intact / Skin is broken but wound not bleeding / Skin is broken and wound bleeding OR mucous membrane contaminated with saliva
questions.text: What is the WHO wound / exposure category?

options.label: Category I
options.who_definition: Animal licks on intact skin, touching or feeding the animal

options.label: Category II
options.who_definition: Minor scratches or abrasions, nibbling of uncovered skin
options.clinical_description: Skin is broken but wound not bleeding

options.label: Category III
options.who_definition: Transdermal bites or scratches, licks on broken skin, contamination of mucous membrane with saliva, exposure to a bat
options.clinical_description: Skin is broken and wound bleeding, OR mucous membrane contaminated with saliva

inline_notes: If exposure was to a bat, PEP for WHO wound category III should be started immediately.
